question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker operate a malicious block node that streams exactly maxBlockItems (800,000) block items per block, where the last item is a crafted BlockProof with a forged previous-hash, bypassing the item-count guard in BlockAssembler.append() while injecting tampered Hashgraph history into the mirror node database?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker control a block node and stream blocks whose pendingCount stays just below maxBlockItems across many BlockItemSet messages, causing BlockAssembler to assemble a block that never triggers the Too-many-block-items exception, allowing tampered block content (altered transaction records) to pass into BlockStreamVerifier.verify() and corrupt the Hashgraph history record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker set maxBlockItems to its minimum allowed value of 1000 via environment variable injection (HIERO_MIRROR_IMPORTER_BLOCK_STREAM_MAXBLOCKITEMS=1000), causing legitimate large blocks to be rejected while a crafted attacker-controlled block node serves minimal 1000-item blocks containing tampered Hashgraph history that passes all downstream verification?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxStreamResponseSize()] Can an unprivileged external attacker operate a malicious block node that sends gRPC responses exactly at the maxStreamResponseSize limit (36 MB), where the oversized payload contains a crafted block with a manipulated BlockProof hash, exploiting the fact that maxInboundMessageSize is cast from DataSize.toBytes() to int without overflow checking, potentially allowing a negative or zero inbound size limit that bypasses the gRPC size guard and injects tampered history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxStreamResponseSize()] Can an unprivileged external attacker configure maxStreamResponseSize to an extremely large value (e.g., Integer.MAX_VALUE MB) via environment variable, causing the int cast of DataSize.toBytes() in BlockNode constructor to overflow to a negative maxInboundMessageSize, which gRPC interprets as unlimited, allowing the attacker's block node to stream arbitrarily large tampered block payloads that overwhelm the verifier and corrupt Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxStreamResponseSize()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node send a stream response just under maxStreamResponseSize (36 MB) containing a block with thousands of duplicate or replayed transaction items, where the sheer volume causes BlockStreamVerifier.verifyHashChain() to be called with a manipulated previousHash that matches a forged chain, effectively rewriting Hashgraph history within the allowed size budget?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxSubscribeAttempts()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious block node deliberately return error status codes (e.g., NOT_AVAILABLE) exactly maxSubscribeAttempts (3) times to trigger onError() and mark all legitimate block nodes inactive, then serve tampered blocks during the readmit window before legitimate nodes are re-admitted, causing the mirror node to ingest forged Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxSubscribeAttempts()] Can an unprivileged external attacker set maxSubscribeAttempts to its minimum value of 1 via environment variable, causing any single transient network error to immediately mark a legitimate block node inactive, forcing the importer to fall back to an attacker-controlled lower-priority block node that serves tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxSubscribeAttempts()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that maxSubscribeAttempts defaults to 3 and the error counter is reset to 0 on success (errors.set(0) in streamBlocks), by interleaving exactly 2 errors then 1 success repeatedly to keep a legitimate node active while simultaneously streaming tampered blocks from a second attacker-controlled node that has higher priority, causing the verifier to process forged history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getReadmitDelay()] Can an unprivileged external attacker configure readmitDelay to its minimum value of 10 seconds via environment variable, then repeatedly trigger maxSubscribeAttempts errors on all legitimate block nodes to cycle them through inactive/readmit states every 10 seconds, creating a continuous window during which only the attacker's block node is active and serving tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getReadmitDelay()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node exploit the readmitDelay window (default 1 minute) by marking all legitimate nodes inactive through deliberate error injection, then serving an entire epoch of tampered blocks during the 1-minute readmit window before any legitimate node is re-admitted, permanently corrupting the Hashgraph history stored in the mirror node database?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getReadmitDelay()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the tryReadmit(boolean force) logic, where force=true bypasses the readmitDelay check, by causing a scenario where all nodes are inactive and the BlockNodeSubscriber force-readmits an attacker-controlled node (the only one with the next block), allowing tampered Hashgraph history to be streamed and verified without waiting for the readmitDelay to expire?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getResponseTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external attacker configure responseTimeout to its minimum value of 100ms via environment variable, causing legitimate block nodes with any network latency above 100ms to always return EMPTY_BLOCK_RANGE from getBlockRange(), making them appear to have no blocks, while the attacker's low-latency malicious block node is always selected and serves tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getResponseTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node exploit the responseTimeout (400ms default) by deliberately delaying server status responses to just over 400ms for all legitimate nodes, causing getBlockRange() to catch the exception and return EMPTY_BLOCK_RANGE for all legitimate nodes, while the attacker's node responds within 400ms and is selected to serve tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getResponseTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that getBlockRange() silently catches all exceptions and returns EMPTY_BLOCK_RANGE (log.error only, no re-throw), by causing a network-level timeout on all legitimate block nodes' status endpoints, so that BlockNodeSubscriber.getNode() only finds the attacker's node with a valid block range, leading to ingestion of tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node send a BlockItemSet where pendingCount integer overflow occurs (pendingCount += blockItems.size() wraps around to a negative value when blockItems.size() is very large), bypassing the maxBlockItems check (pendingCount > streamProperties.getMaxBlockItems() evaluates false for negative pendingCount), allowing an arbitrarily large tampered block to be assembled and passed to BlockStreamVerifier?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that maxBlockItems is checked only in BlockAssembler.append() after accumulation, not per-message, by sending many small BlockItemSet messages each containing fewer items than would trigger the check, accumulating a block with tampered items that collectively exceed what the hash chain verification expects, causing a hash mismatch to be silently swallowed or misattributed?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxStreamResponseSize()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the absence of a lower bound on maxStreamResponseSize (only @NotNull, no @Min) to set it to 1 byte via environment variable, causing all legitimate block nodes to fail with gRPC message-too-large errors (since any real block exceeds 1 byte), marking them all inactive, while the attacker's node serves a crafted 1-byte response that triggers a specific code path allowing tampered history injection?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxStreamResponseSize()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node send a gRPC stream response whose size is exactly maxStreamResponseSize bytes, where the payload is a valid-looking block with a manipulated block number that does not match the filename generated by BlockFile.getFilename(), causing BlockStreamVerifier.verifyBlockNumber() to throw InvalidStreamFileException, incrementing the error counter and eventually marking legitimate nodes inactive while the attacker retries with a corrected block number containing tampered history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxSubscribeAttempts()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that maxSubscribeAttempts has no upper bound constraint, and if set to Integer.MAX_VALUE via environment variable, the errors.incrementAndGet() >= streamProperties.getMaxSubscribeAttempts() check never triggers, meaning a malicious block node can stream unlimited tampered blocks without ever being marked inactive, allowing indefinite Hashgraph history manipulation?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxSubscribeAttempts()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node exploit the race condition in the AtomicInteger errors counter (errors.incrementAndGet() in onError() vs errors.set(0) in streamBlocks success path) by interleaving concurrent streaming calls, causing the error count to be reset mid-increment, preventing legitimate nodes from being marked inactive while the attacker's node continues serving tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getReadmitDelay()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that readmitDelay has no upper bound constraint, and if set to Duration.MAX_VALUE via environment variable, all block nodes that are ever marked inactive are permanently excluded, causing the importer to have no available nodes and halt block ingestion entirely, creating a denial-of-service that prevents legitimate Hashgraph history from being recorded while the attacker prepares a history-rewriting attack?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getResponseTimeout()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that responseTimeout has no upper bound constraint, and if set to a very large value (e.g., Duration.ofDays(1)) via environment variable, the withDeadlineAfter() call in getBlockRange() blocks the calling thread for up to 1 day per node status check, causing the block ingestion pipeline to stall and creating a window for Hashgraph history manipulation via alternative attack vectors?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getMaxBlockItems()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a block node send a block where the BLOCK_HEADER item is followed by exactly maxBlockItems-1 tampered transaction items and then a forged BlockProof, staying just under the limit, so that BlockAssembler.onEndOfBlock() assembles and passes the tampered block to BlockStreamVerifier without triggering the item count exception, resulting in corrupted Hashgraph history being persisted?
